critique.summary: The article presents Antfly, a distributed search engine implemented in Go that integrates full-text search, vector similarity, and graph traversal for multimodal data including text, images, audio, and video. It features built-in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) agents, automatic relationship extraction, and supports distributed consensus and sharding for scalability.
mainPoints: Antfly combines full-text search (BM25), dense and sparse vector search, and graph traversal in a single query over multimodal data.
It includes built-in RAG agents with streaming, multi-turn chat, tool calling, and confidence scoring.
Automatic generation of embeddings, chunking, and graph edges occurs as data is written.
Supports multimodal indexing and search using models like CLIP and CLAP for images, audio, and video.
Offers features like reranking, aggregations, ACID transactions at shard level, document TTL, and S3 storage integration.
Uses a multi-raft distributed architecture with formal verification of critical protocols via TLA+.
Includes SDKs and client libraries for Go, TypeScript, Python, React, and a PostgreSQL extension for integration.
The core server is licensed under Elastic License 2.0, while other components use Apache 2.0.
Provides a web dashboard called Antfarm for interactive exploration of search, RAG, knowledge graphs, and more.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Antfly's features and architecture are consistent with the publicly available information on its official GitHub repository and documentation. The description of its multimodal search capabilities, distributed consensus using Raft, and integration with various ML models aligns with current state-of-the-art technologies in search and AI. The licensing details and community aspects are also verifiable. There is no indication of misinformation or unsupported claims in the article.
considerations: The article does not provide independent third-party evaluations or benchmarks comparing Antfly's performance to other search engines.
Potential users should consider the Elastic License 2.0 restrictions when deploying Antfly in commercial managed services.
The complexity of setting up and maintaining a distributed system like Antfly may require significant technical expertise.
Security and privacy implications of indexing multimodal data and using external ML models are not discussed.
The scalability claims, while supported by architecture descriptions, lack detailed empirical evidence in the article.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the Antfly project, a distributed multimodal search and memory system combining vector DB, Elasticsearch, and graph store functionalities. Users express curiosity about its implementation details, practical features, and usability, especially regarding query capabilities and integration.

comments.author: schmichael
comments.text: As a longtime Raft user (via hashicorp/raft), I'm curious about your Raft implementation! You mention etcd's Raft library, but it isn't natively Multi-Raft. Is your implementation similar to https://tikv.org/deep-dive/scalability/multi-raft/ ? I'd love to hear about your experience implementing and testing it!
comments.analysis: This comment raises a technically relevant question about the Raft implementation, showing understanding of distributed consensus and seeking clarification, which is constructive and likely accurate.

comments.author: thefogman
comments.text:
Interesting project.

I’ve got a project right now, separate vector DB, Elasticsearch, graph store, all for an agent system.

When you say Antfly combines all three, what does that actually look like at query time? Can I write one query that does semantic similarity + full-text + graph traversal together, or is it more like three separate indexes that happen to live in the same binary?

Does it ship with a CLI that's actually good? I’m pivoting away from MCP. Like can I pipe stuff in, run queries, manage indexes from the terminal without needing to write a client? That matters more to me than the MCP server honestly.

And re: Termite + single binary, is the idea that I can just run `antfly swarm`, throw docs and images at it, and have a working local RAG setup with no API keys? If so, that might save me a lot of docker-compose work.

Who's actually running this distributed vs. single-node? Curious what the typical user experience looks like.
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully probes the integration and user experience of the combined system, focusing on practical usage and CLI capabilities, reflecting genuine interest and relevant concerns.

comments.author: didip
comments.text:
in the query_test.go, I don’t see how the hybrid search is being exercised.

For fun I am making hybrid search too and would love to see how you merge the two list (semantic and keyword) and rerank the importance score.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a technical critique and requests clarification on implementation details, indicating a knowledgeable perspective on hybrid search techniques.

comments.author: SkyPuncher
comments.text:
Can you help me understand what type of practical features Graph Traversal unlocks?

I've seen it on a few products and it doesn't click with me how people are using it.
comments.analysis: The comment seeks to understand the practical applications of graph traversal, showing curiosity and a desire for clarification, which is constructive and relevant.

comments.author: prosdev
comments.text: Of course the two most visionary people I worked with at Lytics went and built this. Just in time... this is the vector database I actually need. Termite is the killer feature for me, native ML inference in a single binary means I can stop duct-taping together embedding APIs for my projects. Excited to spend the upcoming weekends hacking on the Antfly ecosystem.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses enthusiasm and appreciation for the project’s features, particularly the native ML inference, reflecting a positive user perspective but less technical detail.

comments.author: Linell
comments.text: This is very interesting! I noticed that your TypeScript SDK link results in a 404: https://antfly.io/docs/sdks -> https://github.com/antflydb/antfly-ts
comments.analysis: This comment provides useful feedback about broken documentation links, which is helpful for project maintainers and accurate.

comments.author: mrprincerawat
comments.text: Was thinking to create something similar, well done!
comments.analysis: A brief positive comment acknowledging the project’s value, though lacking technical depth.

comments.author: jnstrdm05
comments.text:
This looks sick!

Did you build this for yourself?
comments.analysis: An enthusiastic comment with a question about the origin of the project, showing interest but limited technical insight.
